track_description: Background process-optimisation drift on route-stable BF-BOF energy inputs, kept separate from the major route-change methods.
evidence_summary: The steel autonomous track keeps a modest route-stable BF-BOF efficiency drift explicit while leaving the large route-change and CCS effects embodied in the existing steel methods.
derivation_method: Rebaseline the BF-BOF conventional and CCS-transition rows upward on coal, gas, and electricity inputs, then use a single common autonomous track to recover the current pathway behavior during baseline solves.
double_counting_guardrail: Do not apply to scrap EAF or H2-DRI-electric methods. The BF-BOF base rows are explicitly rebaselined so this drift decomposes the legacy trend rather than stacking on top of it.
review_notes: The common BF-BOF drift stays modest relative to the much larger route-change scrap and CCS effects already embodied in pathway methods.
